benchmark_description: Participate in collaborative conversations with diverse partners about grade 2 topics and texts with peers and adults in small and larger groups, following agreed-upon rules for discussions and building on others' talk.

lesson.title: Taking Turns and Talking Together: Rules for Discussion
lesson.intro: Have you ever been in a group where everyone talked at once and nobody could hear anything? Good conversations need rules so that everyone gets a chance to share. Today we will learn the rules for having a great discussion.
lesson.body_md:
## What Is a Collaborative Conversation?

A **collaborative conversation** is when a group works and talks together. Everyone contributes — no single person does all the talking.

## The Rules for Good Discussions

**1. Take turns speaking.**
Wait for your turn. Do not interrupt someone who is talking.

**2. Listen carefully when others speak.**
Look at the speaker. Think about what they are saying. Do not think about your own point only.

**3. Build on others' ideas.**
Say things like:
- *"I agree with __ because..."*
- *"That is a good point. I would add..."*
- *"I see it differently because..."*

**4. Ask clarifying questions.**
If you don't understand, ask:
- *"Can you explain what you mean?"*
- *"Could you give an example?"*

**5. Stay on topic.**
Keep your comments related to what the group is discussing.

**6. Be respectful.**
Even if you disagree, use kind words. You can disagree with an idea without being unkind to the person.

## Why These Rules Matter

When everyone follows the rules, every person's voice is heard and the group learns more together than any one person could learn alone.
lesson.worked_example_md:
**Scenario:** A class is discussing the question: *"Should schools have longer recesses?"*

- Mia says: *"I think yes, because exercise helps us focus better."*
- Jon says: *"I agree with Mia. I also think kids are happier after running around."* (builds on Mia's idea)
- Sam says: *"Can you say more about focusing, Mia?"* (asks a clarifying question)
- Lee says: *"I see it differently. We also need time to learn. Longer recess means less class time."* (disagrees respectfully and stays on topic)

**What went well:** Everyone took turns, built on each other's ideas, asked questions, and stayed on topic. Lee disagreed respectfully.

practice_questions.prompt_md: What should you do when someone else is speaking during a discussion?

choices.label: Talk over them so everyone can hear your idea.
choices.explanation: Talking over someone is rude and breaks the rules for discussion.

choices.label: Listen carefully and wait for your turn.
choices.explanation: Good listeners wait for their turn and pay attention to what the speaker says.

choices.label: Whisper to a friend about something else.
choices.explanation: Whispering is disrespectful and means you are not listening.

choices.label: Raise your hand and start talking immediately.
choices.explanation: Raising your hand is good, but you still need to wait to be called on before speaking.

practice_questions.explanation_md: The rule is to **listen carefully** when others speak and **wait for your turn**.

practice_questions.prompt_md: Which phrase is a good way to **build on** someone else's idea?

choices.label: "That's wrong."
choices.explanation: Just saying 'that's wrong' is not building on an idea and is not respectful.

choices.label: "I agree with your idea, and I would add that..."
choices.explanation: This phrase shows you heard the other person and are adding to their idea.

choices.label: "Let's talk about something else."
choices.explanation: Changing the subject breaks the rule to stay on topic.

choices.label: "My idea is better."
choices.explanation: This is not respectful and does not build on the other person's idea.

practice_questions.explanation_md: **Building on an idea** means connecting your thought to what someone else said and adding to it.

practice_questions.prompt_md: During a discussion, Keisha says something confusing. What should you do?

choices.label: Ignore what she said and share your own idea.
choices.explanation: Ignoring is not listening or collaborating.

choices.label: Laugh at her because it was confusing.
choices.explanation: Laughing at a classmate is unkind and breaks the rule of being respectful.

choices.label: Ask a clarifying question like 'Can you explain what you mean?'
choices.explanation: Asking a clarifying question shows you are listening and want to understand.

choices.label: Ask the teacher to answer for Keisha.
choices.explanation: The group should work together first — asking for clarification is the best step.

practice_questions.explanation_md: **Asking a clarifying question** — like 'Can you explain?' — shows you listened and want to understand.

practice_questions.prompt_md: Which student is **NOT** following the rules for a good discussion?

choices.label: Ana waits for her turn and then shares her idea.
choices.explanation: Ana is following the rules correctly.

choices.label: Ben says 'I agree with what Mia said, and I want to add...'
choices.explanation: Ben is building on an idea — that is a great discussion move.

choices.label: Carlos interrupts and starts talking before the teacher finishes speaking.
choices.explanation: Interrupting breaks the rule of waiting your turn and listening.

choices.label: Dana asks 'Could you give an example of what you mean?'
choices.explanation: Dana is asking a clarifying question — a great discussion move.

practice_questions.explanation_md: **Carlos** is breaking the rules by interrupting — a key rule is to wait for your turn and not interrupt.

practice_questions.prompt_md: Why is it important to **stay on topic** during a discussion?

choices.label: So the teacher will not get angry.
choices.explanation: Staying on topic is about the quality of the discussion, not about avoiding punishment.

choices.label: So the group can go deeper into the topic and learn more together.
choices.explanation: When everyone stays on topic, the group can build understanding together instead of getting confused.

choices.label: So only one person gets to talk.
choices.explanation: Staying on topic is about the subject, not about limiting who speaks.

choices.label: Because the first person to speak gets to choose the topic forever.
choices.explanation: Topics in a discussion are usually set by the teacher or the group, not one person.

practice_questions.explanation_md: **Staying on topic** keeps the conversation focused so the group can build deeper understanding together.

practice_questions.prompt_md: Lena disagrees with what Sam said. What is the best way for her to respond?

choices.label: "You're wrong and that's a silly idea."
choices.explanation: Calling an idea 'silly' is disrespectful and breaks discussion rules.

choices.label: Stay silent so she does not upset Sam.
choices.explanation: It is okay to respectfully disagree — staying silent is not always the answer.

choices.label: "I see it differently. I think... because..."
choices.explanation: This phrase disagrees respectfully while staying on topic and giving a reason.

choices.label: Talk to her neighbor instead of the group.
choices.explanation: Side conversations are disrespectful to the group and break discussion rules.

practice_questions.explanation_md: Saying **'I see it differently because...'** lets Lena disagree respectfully and explain her thinking.

practice_questions.prompt_md: A group is discussing the book *Charlotte's Web*. Marcus says, 'My favorite color is blue.' What rule is Marcus breaking?

choices.label: He is not listening.
choices.explanation: We do not know if Marcus is listening — the issue is what he said.

choices.label: He is not staying on topic.
choices.explanation: Marcus's comment about his favorite color has nothing to do with Charlotte's Web.

choices.label: He is interrupting.
choices.explanation: There is no indication he interrupted — the problem is the off-topic comment.

choices.label: He is being disrespectful.
choices.explanation: His comment is off-topic but not rude or disrespectful.

practice_questions.explanation_md: Marcus is **not staying on topic**. A good rule for discussion is to keep comments connected to what the group is talking about.

practice_questions.prompt_md: During a discussion, only one student — Ty — has been talking for five minutes. No one else has said anything. Which rule has been broken, and what should the group do?

choices.label: No rule has been broken — Ty clearly knows the most.
choices.explanation: In a collaborative discussion, everyone should have a turn — it should not be dominated by one person.

choices.label: The rule of taking turns has been broken; others should share their ideas too.
choices.explanation: Collaborative discussions require everyone to take turns — one person dominating is not collaborative.

choices.label: The rule about staying on topic was broken.
choices.explanation: We do not know if Ty was off topic — the issue is that no one else had a turn.

choices.label: The group should ask the teacher to stop the discussion.
choices.explanation: The group should self-correct — others should ask to share, or someone can invite them.

practice_questions.explanation_md: **Taking turns** is a key rule. When one person dominates, other voices are lost — collaborative means everyone contributes.

practice_questions.prompt_md: A teacher says: *'Build on what your partner said.'* What does this mean?

choices.label: Copy exactly what your partner said.
choices.explanation: Building on means adding to or extending, not copying.

choices.label: Disagree with everything your partner said.
choices.explanation: Building on is about connecting and adding, not automatically disagreeing.

choices.label: Connect your idea to your partner's idea and add more.
choices.explanation: Building on means using what your partner said as a foundation and adding your own thinking.

choices.label: Wait for your partner to finish all their ideas before starting.
choices.explanation: Waiting is respectful, but 'building on' means connecting your idea to theirs.

practice_questions.explanation_md: **Building on** means connecting your idea to what your partner said and adding more — like stacking blocks.

practice_questions.prompt_md: A student observes that when her class follows discussion rules, they always learn more than when they do not. Why might following discussion rules lead to **better learning**?

choices.label: Because the rules make the teacher happy, so the teacher teaches better.
choices.explanation: The rules are for the students' benefit, not about teacher mood.

choices.label: Because when everyone is heard and ideas are built upon, the group develops richer understanding together.
choices.explanation: Collaborative rules allow all perspectives to be shared and combined — the group's understanding grows beyond what any one person knew.

choices.label: Because following rules is always better than not following them.
choices.explanation: This is too vague — the question asks specifically about WHY rules lead to better learning.

choices.label: Because students who follow rules get better grades.
choices.explanation: This is about grades, not about the learning process itself.

practice_questions.explanation_md: **Collaborative rules** create conditions for everyone to share and build on ideas — the group learns more than any one person could alone.

mastery_check.prompt_md: Which of the following is the BEST example of a student following discussion rules?

choices.label: Mia talks for the whole discussion and does not let anyone else share.
choices.explanation: Dominating a discussion breaks the taking-turns rule.

choices.label: Jake says 'I agree with Rosa that the book had a sad ending, and I want to add that the character changed a lot.'
choices.explanation: Jake takes his turn, builds on Rosa's idea, and stays on topic — all key rules.

choices.label: Anna whispers to her friend instead of listening to the speaker.
choices.explanation: Whispering during a discussion is disrespectful and shows she is not listening.

choices.label: Luis changes the topic to his favorite video game.
choices.explanation: Changing the topic breaks the stay-on-topic rule.

mastery_check.explanation_md: Jake follows **multiple rules**: he builds on Rosa's idea, stays on topic, and takes his turn appropriately.

mastery_check.prompt_md: Nia says something during a discussion that you did not understand. What should you say?

choices.label: "That makes no sense."
choices.explanation: This is disrespectful and does not ask for clarification.

choices.label: Say nothing — just pretend you understood.
choices.explanation: Pretending you understood will not help you or the group learn.

choices.label: "Nia, can you tell us more about what you mean?"
choices.explanation: This is a respectful clarifying question that helps you and the whole group understand better.

choices.label: Ask the teacher to explain what Nia meant.
choices.explanation: Asking Nia directly is better — it shows you want to understand her thinking.

mastery_check.explanation_md: Asking **'Can you tell us more?'** is a respectful clarifying question that keeps the collaborative conversation going.

mastery_check.prompt_md: Your class is discussing whether animals should be kept in zoos. You think zoos are good, but your partner said zoos are bad. How should you respond?

choices.label: "You are completely wrong, and zoos are great."
choices.explanation: This is disrespectful and does not build on the partner's idea.

choices.label: Stay silent because you do not want to disagree.
choices.explanation: It is okay to disagree respectfully — sharing your view helps the group.

choices.label: "I understand your point. I see it differently because zoos help endangered animals survive."
choices.explanation: This acknowledges the partner's view and disagrees respectfully with a reason.

choices.label: Change your opinion to match your partner's so there is no disagreement.
choices.explanation: Changing your opinion just to avoid disagreement is not collaborative — honest, respectful disagreement is healthy.

mastery_check.explanation_md: Acknowledging your partner's view and then explaining your different perspective **respectfully** is the best collaborative move.

remediation.prompt_md: During a discussion, you should wait for your ___ before speaking.

choices.label: favorite food
choices.explanation: That has nothing to do with discussions.

choices.label: turn
choices.explanation: Always wait for your turn before speaking in a discussion.

choices.label: friend to leave the room
choices.explanation: That is not a discussion rule.

choices.label: teacher to leave
choices.explanation: That is not a discussion rule.

remediation.explanation_md: In a discussion, always wait for your **turn** before speaking.

remediation.prompt_md: When someone else is talking in a group, you should ___.

choices.label: Talk over them.
choices.explanation: Talking over someone is rude and means you are not listening.

choices.label: Look away and think about something else.
choices.explanation: Looking away shows you are not listening.

choices.label: Listen carefully to what they are saying.
choices.explanation: Listening carefully is a key rule — you need to hear what others say to have a good discussion.

choices.label: Write a note to a friend.
choices.explanation: Writing notes means you are not paying attention to the speaker.

remediation.explanation_md: **Listen carefully** when others are speaking — this is one of the most important discussion rules.

remediation.prompt_md: If you do not understand what someone said in a discussion, you should ___.

choices.label: Ignore what they said.
choices.explanation: Ignoring is not collaborative.

choices.label: Ask them to explain or give an example.
choices.explanation: Asking for clarification helps you understand and shows you are listening.

choices.label: Laugh at them.
choices.explanation: Laughing is unkind and breaks the respectful discussion rule.

choices.label: Leave the discussion.
choices.explanation: Leaving is not a solution — asking for clarification is.

remediation.explanation_md: **Ask for clarification** — like 'Can you explain?' — when you do not understand what someone said.

remediation.prompt_md: Which sentence **builds on** what someone else said?

choices.label: "I like pizza."
choices.explanation: This does not connect to what someone else said.

choices.label: "I agree and I want to add that bees also help make fruit."
choices.explanation: This agrees with a previous speaker and adds new information — it builds on their idea.

choices.label: "Can we stop talking now?"
choices.explanation: This does not build on any idea.

choices.label: "What time is lunch?"
choices.explanation: This is off-topic and does not build on any idea.

remediation.explanation_md: **Building on** an idea means connecting your thought to what someone else said and adding more to it.

remediation.prompt_md: Your class is talking about rainforests. Tomas says, 'I love video games.' What rule did Tomas break?

choices.label: He did not listen.
choices.explanation: We do not know if he was listening — the problem is what he said.

choices.label: He did not stay on topic.
choices.explanation: Video games have nothing to do with rainforests — Tomas went off topic.

choices.label: He interrupted someone.
choices.explanation: There is no sign he interrupted — the issue is the off-topic comment.

choices.label: He was unkind.
choices.explanation: Tomas was not unkind — just off topic.

remediation.explanation_md: Tomas broke the **stay-on-topic** rule. Discussions should stay focused on the subject the group is exploring.
